Name: BINAL DOSHI¶

Sap Id: 53026250002¶

OJT TOPIC : Global Stock Market Analytics-Predicting Market Direction¶

Phase 1¶

In [1]:
import yfinance as yf
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import shapiro

Step 1 : Download NIFTY 50 data (India)

In [2]:
Nifty50 = yf.download("^NSEI", start="2016-01-01", end="2025-12-31")

/tmp/ipykernel_148/2388026088.py:1: FutureWarning: YF.download() has changed argument auto_adjust default to True
  Nifty50 = yf.download("^NSEI", start="2016-01-01", end="2025-12-31")
[*********************100%***********************]  1 of 1 completed
In [3]:
Nifty50.reset_index(inplace=True)

Comment: The index was reset to convert the date index into a regular column, which is required for merging datasets using the Date field.

In [4]:
Nifty50.columns
Out[4]:
MultiIndex([(  'Date',      ''),
            ( 'Close', '^NSEI'),
            (  'High', '^NSEI'),
            (   'Low', '^NSEI'),
            (  'Open', '^NSEI'),
            ('Volume', '^NSEI')],
           names=['Price', 'Ticker'])
In [5]:
Nifty50.columns = Nifty50.columns.droplevel(1)
In [6]:
Nifty50.columns
Out[6]:
Index(['Date', 'Close', 'High', 'Low', 'Open', 'Volume'], dtype='object', name='Price')
In [7]:
Nifty50.rename(columns={
    "Open": "Nifty50_Open",
    "High": "Nifty50_High",
    "Low": "Nifty50_Low",
    "Close": "Nifty50_Close",
    "Adj Close": "Nifty50_AdjClose",
    "Volume": "Nifty50_Volume"
}, inplace=True)

Comment: Compares today’s price with yesterday’s price

In [54]:
master_df = pd.merge(Nifty50, DowJones, on="Date", how="outer")
master_df = pd.merge(master_df, NASDAQ, on="Date", how="outer")
master_df = pd.merge(master_df, HangSeng, on="Date", how="outer")
master_df = pd.merge(master_df, Nikkei225, on="Date", how="outer")
master_df = pd.merge(master_df, DAX, on="Date", how="outer")

master_df.sort_values("Date", inplace=True)
master_df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

master_df.head()

Create Quarter Indicator

In [58]:
master_df['Quarter'] = master_df['Date'].dt.quarter

Create Month Indicator

In [59]:
master_df['Month'] = master_df['Date'].dt.month

Create Year Indicator

In [60]:
master_df['Year'] = master_df['Date'].dt.year

(2606, 40)

Check Normal Distribution

Comment: We want to see whether daily returns behave like a normal (bell-shaped) distribution or not.

In [64]:
from scipy.stats import shapiro

columns = [
    'Nifty50_Daily_Return',
    'DowJones_Daily_Return',
    'NASDAQ_Daily_Return',
    'HangSeng_Daily_Return',
    'Nikkei225_Daily_Return',
    'DAX_Daily_Return'
]

for col in columns:
    stat, p = shapiro(master_df[col].dropna())
    print(col, "p-value:", p)

Nifty50_Daily_Return p-value: 8.94099024021726e-41
DowJones_Daily_Return p-value: 1.4969030780714503e-45
NASDAQ_Daily_Return p-value: 2.2612004530098785e-35
HangSeng_Daily_Return p-value: 1.559156015254688e-27
Nikkei225_Daily_Return p-value: 1.8456149294764425e-34
DAX_Daily_Return p-value: 2.7402479383810763e-36

Daily returns do NOT follow a normal distribution 📌 Decision Rule (Remember This)

p-value ≤ 0.05 → ❌ Not Normal

p-value > 0.05 → ✔ Normal

Since all p-values ≪ 0.05, we reject the normality assumption.

Phase 2¶

In [65]:
master_df.to_csv("Phase1_Master_Data.csv", index=False)
In [66]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

Import the Data

1) Global Indices 10 years Performance Analytics:(For each index (a to f above)¶

Use last 10 years data (Example: 2016-2025)

Create 10-year dataset

In [69]:
df_5y = master_df[
    (master_df['Date'] >= '2016-01-04') &
    (master_df['Date'] <= '2025-12-31')
].copy()


df_5y['Year'] = df_5y['Date'].dt.year
df_5y['Quarter'] = df_5y['Date'].dt.quarter

1(a) Box-Whisker Plot of daily returns by YEAR

In [70]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

markets = [
    ('Nifty50_Daily_Return', 'NIFTY 50', '#1f77b4'),
    ('DowJones_Daily_Return', 'Dow Jones', '#ff7f0e'),
    ('NASDAQ_Daily_Return', 'NASDAQ', '#2ca02c'),
    ('HangSeng_Daily_Return', 'Hang Seng', '#d62728'),
    ('Nikkei225_Daily_Return', 'Nikkei 225', '#9467bd'),
    ('DAX_Daily_Return', 'DAX', '#8c564b')
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, (col, title, color) in zip(axes, markets):
    sns.boxplot(
        x='Year',
        y=col,
        data=df_5y,
        ax=ax,
        color=color
    )
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel('Year')
    ax.set_ylabel('Daily Return')

plt.suptitle(
    "Box-Whisker Plot of Daily Returns by Year (All Markets)",
    fontsize=18
)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()

Comment: Box-Whisker Plot of Daily Returns (2016–2025)

All markets show higher volatility in 2020, visible from wider boxes and many extreme outliers, indicating the impact of the COVID-19 market crash.

NIFTY 50 shows relatively moderate volatility compared to global indices, with returns mostly clustered around zero except during 2020.

Dow Jones and NASDAQ exhibit larger positive and negative outliers, especially NASDAQ, indicating higher risk–return behavior in the US markets.

NASDAQ displays strong recovery after 2020, with wider upper whiskers in later years, reflecting higher positive returns.

Hang Seng shows persistent volatility and weaker performance, with frequent negative outliers even in post-COVID years, indicating prolonged market stress.

Nikkei 225 has stable returns in most years, but with occasional sharp negative outliers, especially around global crisis periods.

DAX demonstrates moderate volatility, with noticeable downside risk during 2020 and gradual stabilization in subsequent years.

Overall, global markets differ in risk characteristics, where US indices are more volatile, Asian markets show mixed behavior, and NIFTY remains comparatively stable.

1(c) Bar Plot of median daily return by YEAR

In [77]:
# Create 2x3 subplot layout
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(20, 10))
fig.suptitle("Median Daily Return by Year (All Markets)", fontsize=18)

markets = [
    ("Nifty50_Daily_Return", "NIFTY 50", "#1f77b4"),     # Blue
    ("DowJones_Daily_Return", "Dow Jones", "#ff7f0e"),   # Orange
    ("NASDAQ_Daily_Return", "NASDAQ", "#2ca02c"),       # Green
    ("HangSeng_Daily_Return", "Hang Seng", "#d62728"),  # Red
    ("Nikkei225_Daily_Return", "Nikkei 225", "#9467bd"),# Purple
    ("DAX_Daily_Return", "DAX", "#8c564b")              # Brown
]

# Define different colors
colors = ["blue", "seagreen", "crimson", "teal""darkorange", "purple",]


# Loop through markets
for ax, (col, title, color) in zip(axes.flatten(), markets):
    median_returns = df_5y.groupby("Year")[col].median()

    median_returns.plot(
        kind="bar",
        ax=ax,
        color=color
    )

    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Year")
    ax.set_ylabel("Median Daily Return")
    ax.axhline(0, linewidth=0.8)  # zero reference line

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()

Comments – Median Daily Return by Year (2016–2025)

NIFTY 50 shows a strong positive median in 2020, indicating a fast recovery after the initial COVID shock, while 2025 shows near-zero to slightly negative median, suggesting recent market consolidation.

Dow Jones performs well in most years, but 2022 shows a clear negative median return, reflecting the impact of inflation concerns and aggressive interest-rate hikes.

NASDAQ records the highest positive median in 2020, highlighting strong gains in technology stocks during the pandemic, followed by a sharp negative median in 2022, indicating tech-sector correction.

Hang Seng displays consistent weakness post-2021, with deep negative median returns in 2022, showing prolonged stress in the Chinese market.

Nikkei 225 shows mixed but generally positive performance, with a small dip around 2020 and strong recovery in later years.

DAX maintains mostly positive median returns, indicating relative stability of the European market, with minor slowdowns during global uncertainty.

Overall, US markets show higher return potential but higher volatility, while Asian markets exhibit uneven recovery, and NIFTY remains comparatively resilient.

1(d) Heat Map by YEAR and QUARTER

In [78]:
# Create 2x3 subplot layout
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(20, 10))
fig.suptitle("Heat Map of Median Daily Returns (Year vs Quarter)", fontsize=18)

markets = [
    ("Nifty50_Daily_Return", "NIFTY 50"),
    ("DowJones_Daily_Return", "Dow Jones"),
    ("NASDAQ_Daily_Return", "Nasdaq"),
    ("HangSeng_Daily_Return", "Hang Seng"),
    ("Nikkei225_Daily_Return", "Nikkei 225"),
    ("DAX_Daily_Return", "DAX")
]

# Different colormaps
cmaps = ["RdYlGn", "viridis", "magma", "YlGnBu", "Spectral", "crest"]

# Loop through markets
for ax, (col, title), cmap in zip(axes.flatten(), markets, cmaps):
    pivot_table = master_df.pivot_table(
        values=col,
        index="Year",
        columns="Quarter",
        aggfunc="median"
    )

    sns.heatmap(
        pivot_table,
        ax=ax,
        annot=True,
        fmt=".2f",
        cmap=cmap,   # different color for each
        center=0,
        cbar=False
    )

    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Quarter")
    ax.set_ylabel("Year")

# Add one common colorbar (based on first heatmap)
cbar = fig.colorbar(
    axes[0, 0].collections[0],
    ax=axes,
    orientation="vertical",
    fraction=0.02,
    pad=0.02
)
cbar.set_label("Median Daily Return")

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()

2. Global Indices – Correlation Analysis¶

2(a) Correlation Matrix of 10 years daily returns

In [85]:
# Calculate 10-Year Correlation Heatmap
returns_cols = [
    'Nifty50_Daily_Return',
    'DowJones_Daily_Return',
    'NASDAQ_Daily_Return',
    'HangSeng_Daily_Return',
    'Nikkei225_Daily_Return',
    'DAX_Daily_Return'
]

corr_10y = master_df[returns_cols].corr()

# Calculate 2025 Correlation Heatmap
corr_2025 = master_df[master_df['Year'] == 2025][returns_cols].corr()

# -------- Plotting --------
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(18, 7))
fig.suptitle("Correlation Matrix of Global Market Daily Returns", fontsize=18)

# 10-Year Correlation Heatmap
sns.heatmap(
    corr_10y,
    annot=True,
    cmap="RdYlGn",   # changed colormap
    center=0,
    fmt=".2f",
    ax=axes[0],
    cbar=False
)
axes[0].set_title("10-Year Daily Returns Correlation")

# 2025 Correlation Heatmap
sns.heatmap(
    corr_2025,
    annot=True,
    cmap="RdYlGn",   # changed colormap
    center=0,
    fmt=".2f",
    ax=axes[1],
    cbar=False
)
axes[1].set_title("2025 Daily Returns Correlation")

# One common colorbar
cbar = fig.colorbar(
    axes[0].collections[0],
    ax=axes,
    orientation="vertical",
    fraction=0.03,
    pad=0.02
)
cbar.set_label("Correlation Coefficient")

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.93])
plt.show()

In [86]:
returns_cols = [
    'Nifty50_Daily_Return',
    'DowJones_Daily_Return',
    'NASDAQ_Daily_Return',
    'HangSeng_Daily_Return',
    'Nikkei225_Daily_Return',

plt.title("Correlation Matrix – 10 Years")
plt.show()

Comment: • Dow Jones and NASDAQ have the strongest correlation (0.83), meaning they move very closely.

• DAX shows moderate correlation with Dow Jones and NASDAQ.

• NIFTY 50 has a moderate relationship with other global markets.

• Hang Seng and Nikkei 225 show lower correlation with the other indices.

• Overall, correlations are positive but not perfect, allowing diversification.

2(b) Correlation Matrix of 2025 daily returns

In [87]:
sns.heatmap(
    master_df[master_df['Year'] == 2025][returns_cols].corr(),
    annot=True,
    cmap='magma'
)
plt.title("Correlation Matrix – Year 2025")
plt.show()

3. Pre-Post Covid Performance Analytics-time required for a market to come back To pre-covid level¶

In [88]:
def covid_recovery_time(df, price_col):
    pre = df[df['Date'] < '2020-02-01']
    peak_price = pre[price_col].max()
    peak_date = pre.loc[pre[price_col].idxmax(), 'Date']

    post = df[df['Date'] >= '2020-02-01']
    recovery_date = post[post[price_col] >= peak_price]['Date'].min()

    days = (recovery_date - peak_date).days
    return days
In [89]:
covid_recovery_time(master_df, 'Nifty50_Close')
Out[89]:
300
In [90]:
covid_recovery_time(master_df, 'NASDAQ_Close')
Out[90]:
12
In [91]:
covid_recovery_time(master_df, 'Nikkei225_Close')
Out[91]:
766
In [92]:
covid_recovery_time(master_df, 'DAX_Close')
Out[92]:
18
In [93]:
covid_recovery_time(master_df, 'HangSeng_Close')
Out[93]:
nan

4. NIFTY 50 DAILY MOVEMENT – PRE-MODELING WORK¶

a) Define Nifty_Open_Dir

1 → Nifty opens higher than yesterday’s close (positive opening)

0 → Otherwise

In [94]:
# If today's Nifty Open > yesterday's Close → 1 else 0
master_df['Nifty_Open_Dir'] = (
    master_df['Nifty50_Open'] > master_df['Nifty50_Close'].shift(1)
).astype(int)
In [95]:
master_df[["Date", "Nifty50_Open", "Nifty50_Close", "Nifty_Open_Dir"]].head()
Out[95]:
Date Nifty50_Open Nifty50_Close Nifty_Open_Dir
0 2016-01-04 7924.549805 7791.299805 0
1 2016-01-05 7828.399902 7784.649902 1
2 2016-01-06 7788.049805 7741.000000 1
3 2016-01-07 7673.350098 7568.299805 0
4 2016-01-08 7611.649902 7601.350098 1

Insight: Today’s Opening price is compared with yesterday’s Closing price. The target variable converts the problem into a binary classification task, representing whether the market opens positive or negative.

b) Table of % of Nifty_Open_Dir=1 by year

Meaning : Shows how often Nifty opens positive each year.

In [96]:
# Percentage of positive openings per year
open_dir_pct = master_df.groupby('Year')['Nifty_Open_Dir'].mean() * 100

open_dir_pct
Out[96]:
Nifty_Open_Dir
Year
2016 66.923077
2017 74.615385
2018 70.384615
2019 69.230769
2020 70.610687
2021 71.647510
2022 59.230769
2023 67.307692
2024 64.503817
2025 59.003831

Comment:

74.6% in 2017 → Very strong year with frequent positive openings.

2022 (~59.2%) → Fewer positive openings → weaker or uncertain market.

c) % of NEGATIVE openings per year

In [97]:
neg_open_dir_pct = 100 - open_dir_pct
neg_open_dir_pct
Out[97]:
Nifty_Open_Dir
Year
2016 33.076923
2017 25.384615
2018 29.615385
2019 30.769231
2020 29.389313
2021 28.352490
2022 40.769231
2023 32.692308
2024 35.496183
2025 40.996169

Comment: Negative Nifty openings were lowest in 2017, indicating strong bullish sentiment, while 2022 and 2025 show the highest negative opening percentages, reflecting increased market uncertainty and volatility.

d) Visualize/summarize global indices including VIX for 2 categories of Nifty_Open_Dir

This checks:

“When Nifty opens positive vs negative, how do global markets behave?”

This helps in feature selection for modeling.

In [98]:
# Lagged Dow Jones return (yesterday's return)
master_df['DJI_Return_Lag1'] = master_df['DowJones_Daily_Return'].shift(1)

# Box plot: Dow Jones lagged return vs Nifty opening direction
sns.boxplot(
    x='Nifty_Open_Dir',
    y='DJI_Return_Lag1',
    data=master_df
)

plt.title("Lagged Dow Jones Return vs Nifty Opening Direction")
plt.xlabel("Nifty Open Direction (0 = Down, 1 = Up)")
plt.ylabel("Dow Jones Return (Lag 1)")
plt.show()

Comment: • The chart compares yesterday’s Dow Jones return with today’s Nifty opening direction.

• When the Dow Jones return is positive, Nifty is more likely to open up.

• When the Dow Jones return is negative, Nifty is more likely to open down.

Percentage of Positive Opening Direction by Year¶

In [99]:
markets = [
    'DowJones',
    'NASDAQ',
    'HangSeng',
    'Nikkei225',
    'DAX'
]

for market in markets:
    master_df[f'{market}_Open_Dir'] = (
        master_df[f'{market}_Open'] > master_df[f'{market}_Close'].shift(1)
    ).astype(int)
In [100]:
# Create 2x3 subplot layout
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(20, 10))
fig.suptitle("Percentage of Positive Opening Direction by Year", fontsize=18)

markets = [
    ("Nifty_Open_Dir", "NIFTY 50", "#1f77b4"),
    ("DowJones_Open_Dir", "Dow Jones", "#ff7f0e"),
    ("NASDAQ_Open_Dir", "NASDAQ", "#2ca02c"),
    ("HangSeng_Open_Dir", "Hang Seng", "#d62728"),
    ("Nikkei225_Open_Dir", "Nikkei 225", "#9467bd"),
    ("DAX_Open_Dir", "DAX", "#8c564b")
]

# Loop through markets
for ax, (col, title, color) in zip(axes.flatten(), markets):
    open_dir_pct = master_df.groupby("Year")[col].mean() * 100

    ax.bar(
        open_dir_pct.index,
        open_dir_pct.values,
        color=color,
        alpha=0.8
    )

    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Year")
    ax.set_ylabel("Positive Opening (%)")
    ax.axhline(50, linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8)  # neutrality line
    ax.grid(True, axis='y')  # grid only on y-axis for bar chart

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()

Comment: Most markets show more positive openings than negative ones in normal years. In 2022, all markets show a clear drop in positive openings, showing market uncertainty. After 2022, positive openings improve again, especially in NIFTY, NASDAQ, Nikkei, and DAX, indicating recovery in market sentiment.

from google.colab import files
f = files.upload()

# Convert ipynb to html
import subprocess
file0 = list(f.keys())[0]
_ = subprocess.run(["pip", "install", "nbconvert"])
_ = subprocess.run(["jupyter", "nbconvert", file0, "--to", "html"])

# download the html
files.download(file0[:-5]+"html")
